turns.student: Hi, I'm here to help you. What's been going on?
turns.patient: I've had this persistent cough for about five days now. It started out dry and has turned into a productive cough with yellowish-green sputum.

turns.student: That sounds really uncomfortable. I can tell this has been weighing on you.
turns.patient: It's been frustrating me, to be honest. The OTC cough syrup hasn't helped much at all.

turns.student: I want to make sure we don't miss anything important. When these episodes happen, does your body react in any other way?
turns.patient: I've also been experiencing some difficulty breathing and wheezing when I cough or exert myself. It feels like my chest is tight too.

turns.student: I know it can be scary to think about, but do you notice any sweating or feeling faint during these episodes?
turns.patient: I have been having night sweats, soaking through my pajamas every single night for four nights now. It's been really concerning and frustrating because I don't understand what's causing it.

turns.student: Thank you for sharing that. I really appreciate your honesty. Does the discomfort stay in one place or does it spread anywhere?
turns.patient: It feels like it's centered around my chest, but sometimes it spreads to my throat and makes swallowing uncomfortable. The wheezing also seems to come from my lungs, especially when I cough.

turns.student: It must be frightening when symptoms like these happen. Any other areas of your body affected?
turns.patient: To be honest, just the fatigue has been bothering me a lot. I've missed three days of work because I'm just so exhausted all the time, and it's affecting my mood too.

turns.student: I'm concerned about you. Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Even minor changes?
turns.patient: I haven't really noticed any swelling that I'd call significant. My face doesn't feel puffy or anything like that, and I don't see any swelling around my ankles or hands either, but to be honest, I'm not really sure what to look for with something like that.

turns.student: Sometimes when people are going through something like this, they also feel nauseous or have stomach upset. Has that happened to you?
turns.patient: Honestly, no, I haven't experienced any nausea or stomach issues related to my symptoms. But to be fair, I have been taking dextromethorphan for a week now with minimal relief, so it's possible that could be contributing to some discomfort or something.

turns.student: I can see this is taking a toll on you. How has your energy been? Your appetite?
turns.patient: My energy's really low, to be honest. I've lost my appetite completely - even thinking about food makes me feel tired.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers or night sweats? I know it might seem unrelated, but it helps me understand the full picture.
turns.patient: I've actually been experiencing a low-grade fever, around 38.2°C (100.8°F), and it's mostly worse in the evenings. The fever hasn't been super high, but it's been there consistently for the past few days.

turns.student: Your health really matters to me as your care provider. Can you tell me about any other medical conditions you've been diagnosed with?
turns.patient: I have seasonal allergies, which means I get allergic rhinitis every year during certain times of the year. I take over-the-counter cetirizine when it flares up. That's really my only ongoing condition that I'm aware of.

turns.student: I know medications can be a hassle. Are you taking everything as prescribed, or has anything been hard to keep up with?
turns.patient: I've actually been pretty good about following the dextromethorphan instructions, but it's just not giving me the relief I need. The cetirizine for my allergies is something I take as needed, and I've been using it a bit more frequently during this allergy season since several of my students have had upper respiratory infections recently.

turns.student: I don't want to pry, but understanding your lifestyle really helps me help you. How do you eat on a typical day?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've been too tired and not hungry enough to worry about cooking or even eating much of anything. Usually, I'm an elementary school teacher, so my days are pretty busy with planning lessons and teaching, but lately, just getting out of bed in the morning has felt like a chore. When I do eat, it's mostly just whatever is easy, like leftovers or takeout.

turns.student: It sounds like your life keeps you really busy. Do you get a chance to exercise or stay active?
turns.patient: Honestly, exercise and staying active have been really challenging for me lately. I usually enjoy going for walks during my free time, but even that feels like too much effort right now. Just getting through my days has become exhausting, so regular exercise is just not something I've been able to prioritize.

turns.student: I hate to ask this, but it's really important — has anyone in your family had serious health problems?
turns.patient: My mom actually has asthma. It's not too severe, but she's dealt with it for a while now.

turns.student: I'm so sorry to hear that. That must have been incredibly difficult. Do you worry about your own health because of it?
turns.patient: I do worry about my own health, especially when I see how my mom has managed her asthma over the years. It makes me a bit anxious, but I try not to think too much about it and focus on taking care of myself instead.

turns.student: Given everything you've been through, I want to be thorough. Do you drink alcohol? How do you cope with stress?
turns.patient: I do have a glass or two of wine on the weekends, usually one night out with my partner and maybe another quiet night at home. To be honest, it's been harder to unwind lately because I'm so exhausted all the time, but when I do drink, it's just to relax and take my mind off things for a bit.

turns.student: I want to make sure we're being thorough. Do you smoke or use any substances?
turns.patient: No, I've never smoked. And as for using other substances, no, I don't have anything like that going on.

turns.student: Is there anything you've been hesitant to tell me? I promise there's no judgment here.
turns.patient: I appreciate your non-judgmental attitude and willingness to listen. To be honest, I was initially hesitant to bring up my night sweats because I thought it might sound silly or unrelated to my cough, but you've created a safe space for me to share everything, so thank you for that.

turns.student: Before we wrap up, is there anything else you're worried about that you want to share?
turns.patient: Actually, now that I think about it, I have had some recurrent wheezing episodes since I was a kid. They used to happen with colds or when I exerted myself, but they never really got formally diagnosed. It's been happening around 2-3 times per year, and it's always triggered by something like a cold or exercise.
